name: La Peña Reservoir Loop from Murillo de Gállego
name_original: Resiguiendo el Embalse de la Peña — desde Murillo de Gállego
description: Cycle 15.2 miles (24.4 km) around La Peña Reservoir from Murillo de Gállego, enjoying moderate terrain and views of Mallos de Riglos.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Murillo De Gállego offers routes through the dramatic landscapes of the Aragonese Pre-Pyrenees. The region is characterized by towering reddish rock formations known as the Mallos de Riglos, the scenic Gállego River, and diverse pre-Pyrenean terrain. Cyclists will encounter varied topography, including open paths, narrow gorges, and challenging climbs, marking a geological boundary between the Ebro depression and the Pyrenees. Best touring cycling routes around Murillo De Gállego The most popular touring cycling route is La Peña Reservoir Loop from Murillo de Gállego, a 15.1 miles (24.4 km) trail that takes 1 hour 39 minutes to complete, offering views of the reservoir and river. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Mallos de Riglos – Murillo de Gállego loop from Murillo de Gállego, a difficult 17.2 miles (27.6 km) path. This route provides continuous vistas of the iconic Mallos de Riglos. Local touring cyclists also love the Las Piedras de Agüero – Mallos de Agüero loop from Riglos-Concilio, a 37.8 miles (60.8 km) trail leading through the diverse pre-Pyrenean terrain and past the Mallos de Agüero, often completed in about 6 hours 4 minutes. Touring cycling around Murillo De Gállego is defined by the majestic Mallos de Riglos, the Gállego River valley, and the rugged Aragonese Pre-Pyrenees. The network offers options for moderate to difficult ability levels. The routes in Murillo De Gállego are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from more than 20 reviews. More than 300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Murillo De Gállego's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Murillo de Gállego?
A: The region offers a diverse range of terrain, characteristic of the Aragonese Pre-Pyrenees. You'll encounter open paths, challenging climbs, and narrow gorges like the Foz de Escalete. The landscape is defined by its dramatic geological features, including the towering Mallos de Riglos and the scenic Gállego River valley.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of touring cycling routes in Murillo de Gállego?
A: Murillo de Gállego provides a variety of routes catering to different skill levels. While there are no easy routes listed, you can find 14 moderate routes and 29 difficult routes. This ensures options for both experienced cyclists and those looking for a more challenging adventure.

Q: What natural landmarks and viewpoints can I expect to see while cycling in Murillo de Gállego?
A: Cyclists will be treated to continuous stunning vistas. Key natural landmarks include the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/349405" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñas de Riglos&lt;/a&gt; (Mallos de Riglos), the scenic Gállego River, and the rugged Aragonese Pre-Pyrenees. You can also find viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/324002" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Riglos Griffon Vulture Viewing Area&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/349408" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cielo - Panoramic View&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions accessible by bike?
A: Yes, beyond the natural beauty, you can explore historical and cultural sites. Notable attractions include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/793375" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marcuello Castle&lt;/a&gt;, which offers spectacular views. The village of Murillo de Gállego itself features a charming urban center with traditional architecture and the monumental Church of El Salvador, an icon of Aragonese Romanesque art.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Murillo de Gállego?
A: The region is particularly well-suited for outdoor activities, including touring cycling, from &lt;strong&gt;March to October&lt;/strong&gt;. This period benefits from the influence of the Mediterranean climate, offering generally favorable weather conditions for exploring the routes.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available in Murillo de Gállego?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e975096810/la-pena-reservoir-loop-from-murillo-de-gallego" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Peña Reservoir Loop from Murillo de Gállego&lt;/a&gt; and the more challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31500897" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mallos de Riglos – Murillo de Gállego loop from Murillo de Gállego&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Murillo de Gállego?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from more than 20 reviews. Cyclists often praise the continuous stunning vistas, particularly of the iconic Mallos de Riglos and the Gállego River valley, as well as the varied and rewarding pre-Pyrenean terrain.

Q: Is there parking available near the cycling routes in Murillo de Gállego?
A: Murillo de Gállego is easily accessible and serves as a strategic location for outdoor activities. While specific parking areas for each trailhead are not detailed, the village generally offers accessibility for visitors arriving by car.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Murillo de Gállego?
A: While the guide primarily lists moderate to difficult routes, the region does offer services that can support family cycling. For instance, there are options for e-bike rentals, even for children, which can make some routes more accessible for families. It's advisable to check individual route details for suitability.

Q: Can I rent bikes in Murillo de Gállego?
A: Yes, Murillo de Gállego provides specialized services for cyclists. You can find mountain bike and e-bike rentals, including options for children, making it convenient to explore the area even if you don't bring your own equipment.

Q: Are there any long-distance touring cycling routes near Murillo de Gállego?
A: The region offers routes that can be combined or extended for longer distances. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40005990" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Piedras de Agüero – Mallos de Agüero loop from Riglos-Concilio&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 37.8-mile (60.8 km) trail that explores diverse pre-Pyrenean terrain, suitable for a longer day out.

Q: What are some of the more challenging touring cycling routes in the area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31500897" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mallos de Riglos – Murillo de Gállego loop from Murillo de Gállego&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38154206" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foz de Escalete – View of Los Mallos loop from Murillo de Gállego&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gains and demanding terrain, providing a rewarding experience.
tips: Bridge that crosses the Embalse de la Peña from the town of Murillo de Gállego. The bridge shows the turquoise waters of the reservoir and where it reaches the small island in the middle of the swamp; where there is the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Peña.

Triste is a town that is right next to the Peña reservoir, from where it fills the waters of the Río Gallego. The town is surrounded to the north by the Sierra de Santa Isabel and to the south by the Sierra de Loarre.
Road that goes from the town of Murillo de Gállego towards the Embalse de Peña, from where you can see Mallos de Riglos. The immense red walls and pinnacles are a must-see.

faqs.question: What is the starting point for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts from Murillo de Gállego, a village known as a hub for outdoor activities. While specific parking details aren't provided, Murillo de Gállego is the primary access point for this trail.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the La Peña Reservoir cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is mostly paved, with approximately 20.6 km of asphalt surfaces including national roads and regular roads. However, be prepared for some unpaved sections, totaling around 3.29 km, which may include gravel or dirt paths.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as 'moderate' in difficulty and requires 'good fitness'. While it's generally accessible for touring cyclists, beginners might find the distance and elevation gain challenging. The area around Murillo de Gállego also offers more challenging rides for experienced cyclists.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll enjoy stunning natural beauty, including the turquoise waters of the La Peña Reservoir. Notable highlights include the Bridge Over the Embalse de la Peña, the village of Triste, and impressive View of Mallos de Riglos.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle Following the La Peña Reservoir?
faqs.answer: The region generally offers pleasant cycling conditions during spring and autumn, when temperatures are milder. Summer can be hot, while winter might bring colder weather, though the specific best time depends on personal preference for temperature and crowd levels.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits to cycle near La Peña Reservoir?
faqs.answer: No, there is no mention of special permits or entrance fees required for cycling this route in Aragon, Spain. You can enjoy the trail freely.

faqs.question: Is the route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The provided information does not explicitly state whether this specific cycling route is dog-friendly. It's always best to check local regulations or signage regarding dogs on trails in the Murillo de Gállego area.

faqs.question: Does this route follow any other established trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other established paths. Approximately 20% of the route follows the Camino de Santiago por Huesca, 20% follows the Sendero de la Calzada Romana, and another 20% is part of the Via Sancti Martini - Chemin de Saragosse. You'll also find smaller sections of the Circular de Riglos and Camino Las Huertas.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the trail?
faqs.answer: Murillo de Gállego is a charming village with traditional stone houses. As a hub for outdoor sports, it likely offers options for cafes, restaurants, and accommodation suitable for cyclists. The village of Triste, also on the route, is another point of interest.

faqs.question: Where can I rent a bike in Murillo de Gállego?
faqs.answer: Murillo de Gállego is known as an adventure sports hub, so it's highly probable that bike rental services are available in the village, especially for touring bikes. It's advisable to check with local businesses upon arrival or search online beforehand.

faqs.question: Are there any specific viewpoints of the Mallos de Riglos on this route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route provides impressive views of the Mallos de Riglos, the dramatic red rock formations that dominate the landscape. One of the highlights specifically mentions a View of Mallos de Riglos.
